title: Artikel: interrogativ und demonstrativ (Interrogative and demonstrative articles)
description: Understanding the difference between interrogative articles (welch-) used for asking "which?" and demonstrative articles (dies-) used for pointing out "this/that."

sections.title: Understanding the Concept: Interrogative vs. Demonstrative
sections.description: Learn the difference between asking for a specific item and pointing out a specific item.

definitions.description: This is used to ask for a specific person or thing out of a group. In English, it translates to "Which...?"
This is used to point out a specific person or thing. In English, it translates to "This..." or "This one."

golden_rule.title: The golden rule for A1 learners
golden_rule.description: The ending of "welch-" must match the ending of "dies-" and the definite article (der/die/das).

sections.title: 1. The Nominative Case (Subject)
sections.description: The Nominative case is used for the subject of the sentence (the person or thing doing the action or being described).

genders.example_question_english: Which pair of pants is that?

sections.title: 2. The Accusative Case (Direct Object)
sections.description: The Accusative case is used for the direct object (the thing being acted upon). Notice that only the masculine gender changes compared to the Nominative.

genders.ending: These stay exactly the same as in the Nominative case (-es, -e, -e)

sections.title: 3. The Dative Case (Indirect Object/Prepositions)
sections.description: The Dative case is used after certain prepositions (like mit, von, zu, aus, bei) or for indirect objects.

genders.example_question_english: To which doctor are you going?

genders.note: This is often confusing for beginners because it looks masculine, but in Dative, feminine becomes der
genders.example_question_english: With which [female] friend are you going?

sections.title: Why do we use these endings?
sections.description: German uses these specific endings to provide "signals." Because word order in German can be flexible, these endings tell the listener exactly who is doing what.

signals.description: The -er in Welcher tells you the noun is masculine (der). The -es in Welches tells you it is neutral (das).
If you see Welchen, you immediately know the masculine noun is the object of the sentence (Accusative) or used with a Dative plural noun.

sections.title: Summary Tip for A1
sections.description: Think of Welch- and Dies- as "mirror words." Whatever ending you put on the question word usually mirrors the ending on the answer word and the definite article:
